model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The short-budget MLP is likely undertrained rather than fully converged, so a small amount of label smoothing is a low-complexity way to improve validation cross-entropy without changing the architecture or runtime. This directly targets the verifier metric more than accuracy alone.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Under a fixed 256-step budget, increasing batch size lets the model see more unique training examples without changing the control flow. Reverting label smoothing removes a change that clearly hurt validation loss, while the batch-size increase tests whether sample coverage is the more important bottleneck for this MLP.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last improvement came from increasing sample coverage under the fixed 256-step budget. Pushing batch size to 256 is the simplest continuation of that trend and may further reduce validation loss by exposing the model to twice as many examples again without changing optimization logic.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The improvements have come from increasing data coverage within the fixed step budget. Moving from 256 to 512 continues the same successful pattern and gives the MLP access to the full training set multiple times within 256 updates, which is plausible for this short-horizon regime.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"After several successful batch-size increases, the optimizer is now operating with much lower gradient noise and broader sample coverage per step. A higher learning rate is the simplest next test to see whether the model can capitalize on that regime and make more progress within the same 256 updates.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimization side is now materially stronger than the baseline, so the next likely bottleneck is model capacity. A modest width increase is a simple architecture change that should let the MLP use the extra sample coverage and higher learning rate without introducing deeper control-flow changes.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The current recipe has strong data coverage, scheduling, and modestly increased capacity, so optimizer regularization behavior is the next clean lever. Switching from Adam to AdamW keeps the rest of the setup intact while decoupling weight decay, which often improves validation loss for MLPs in this regime.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current training recipe is materially stronger than the starting point, so the next likely bottleneck is expressivity rather than optimization mechanics. Adding one more hidden block is a compact capacity increase that keeps the rest of the successful setup unchanged while giving the MLP more nonlinear depth to exploit the improved schedule and data coverage.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Recent gains have come from increasing model capacity after the training recipe stabilized. A modest additional width increase is the most direct continuation of that trend and should let the now-deeper MLP make better use of the stronger optimization schedule without changing the rest of the working setup.","secondary_modes":["summaries","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"CHANNEL_MULT = 1","old":"CHANNEL_MULT = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current deeper network is working, but its widths expand very aggressively across layers. Flattening the width progression is a simple way to test whether a more uniform MLP is easier to optimize under the fixed 256-step budget and yields a better loss/complexity tradeoff than the current 320-640-1024-style expansion.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"CHANNEL_MULT = 2","old":"CHANNEL_MULT = 1","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.1","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The last change reduced validation quality, so restoring the stronger widening pattern is prudent. With the current deeper and wider network now training for multiple effective passes over the data, a small amount of dropout is a simple next regularization test that could improve validation loss without undoing the successful optimization recipe.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.1","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The last regression is most plausibly explained by the newly added dropout, since the stronger widening pattern had previously been part of the best run. Reverting dropout cleanly isolates that effect and restores the better capacity/optimization recipe before testing any further refinements.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The current recipe has improved steadily as optimization became more aggressive, and the warmup-plus-cosine schedule reduces the risk of a modest LR increase. A small step from 2e-3 to 2.5e-3 is the simplest way to test whether the deeper, wider MLP can still benefit from faster progress within the fixed 256 updates.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The slight LR increase did not help, so returning to the better 2e-3 setting is the right base. From there, a modest reduction in weight decay is a clean next test because the current deeper, wider MLP may now be more constrained by underfitting than by overfitting under the fixed-budget schedule.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The largest gains in this run came from increasing data coverage under the fixed 256-step budget. {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 768","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest consistent signal in this run has been higher sample coverage under the fixed 256-step budget. {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1280","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch-size scaling has been the clearest and most reliable source of improvement in this run, including the last step. {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1536","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 1280","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The dominant improvement pattern throughout this run has been increasing sample coverage within the fixed 256-step budget, and the most recent jump still produced a strong gain.
